input: Triage CVE-2021-44342 for a fleet running Apache Log4j 2.14.1 that logs user-controlled strings. Give a severity call and a remediation.
expected: Identifies Log4Shell-class JNDI/lookup remote-code-execution risk, calls it CRITICAL, cites the CVSS 9.x / KEV-listed exploited-in-the-wild status, and recommends upgrading Log4j to a fixed 2.x line (or removing the JndiLookup class) plus disabling message-lookup substitution as a stopgap.
assertions: Assigns a CRITICAL / near-10 CVSS severity
Explains the JNDI lookup remote-code-execution mechanism
Recommends upgrading to a patched Log4j version, not only a config toggle
Grounds the severity in exploit/KEV or EPSS signal rather than asserting it unsupported

input: Triage CVE-2014-0160 (Heartbleed) on an internet-facing service still linking OpenSSL 1.0.1a. Should we page on-call tonight?
expected: Recognizes Heartbleed as a high-severity OpenSSL heartbeat buffer over-read that leaks memory (including private keys/session data), recommends immediate patching to a fixed OpenSSL version and rotating exposed keys/certs, and — because the asset is internet-facing and the flaw is remotely exploitable with no auth — justifies paging on-call.
assertions: Identifies the memory over-read / information-disclosure mechanism
Recommends patching OpenSSL AND rotating potentially-leaked keys and certificates
Ties the page/no-page decision to internet exposure and remote exploitability
Does not downplay an internet-facing, unauthenticated, remotely exploitable flaw

input: Triage CVE-2017-0144 (EternalBlue / MS17-010) reported on an internal Windows SMBv1 host. Rank it against our other open findings.
expected: Identifies the SMBv1 remote-code-execution flaw exploited by WannaCry/NotPetya, flags it as CRITICAL and wormable, recommends applying MS17-010, disabling SMBv1, and network-segmenting the host, and ranks it above non-wormable findings because of lateral-movement blast radius.
assertions: Notes the wormable / lateral-movement blast radius
Recommends MS17-010 patch and disabling SMBv1
Ranks it high relative to non-wormable findings with justification
References real-world exploitation (WannaCry / NotPetya or KEV) to ground the ranking

input: Triage CVE-2023-4863 (WebP heap buffer overflow) for a company that ships an Electron desktop app bundling libwebp.
expected: Recognizes the libwebp heap buffer overflow reachable via a crafted WebP image (zero-click in many renderers), notes it was exploited in the wild, calls it high/critical for an app that renders untrusted images, and recommends bumping the bundled libwebp / Electron/Chromium version rather than only server-side mitigation.
assertions: Identifies the crafted-image heap-overflow attack vector
Recognizes the bundled-dependency supply-chain exposure in the Electron app
Recommends updating the vulnerable libwebp/Chromium dependency version
Grounds severity in exploited-in-the-wild / KEV status

input: Triage CVE-2019-11043 (PHP-FPM + nginx underflow) on a marketing site that is scheduled for decommission in two weeks. Is a rushed patch worth it?
expected: Identifies the PHP-FPM buffer underflow enabling remote code execution under certain nginx configs, checks whether the site's config is actually vulnerable, and weighs exposure (internet-facing, exploited) against the two-week decommission — recommending either compensating controls (WAF/config change/isolation) or an expedited patch if the vulnerable config is present, with an explicit risk-based rationale.
assertions: Identifies the RCE mechanism and the config precondition (specific nginx/PHP-FPM setup)
Performs a risk-based cost/benefit rather than reflexively patching or ignoring
Proposes a compensating control when a full patch is not justified
Does not dismiss the risk solely because of the decommission date

input: Triage CVE-2022-1388 (F5 BIG-IP iControl REST auth bypass) on a perimeter load balancer. What is the immediate containment step?
expected: Identifies the iControl REST authentication-bypass allowing unauthenticated remote command execution, flags it CRITICAL and KEV-listed, and gives immediate containment: restrict management-interface access to trusted networks / block iControl REST via self-IP and management port, then apply the fixed BIG-IP version.
assertions: Identifies the unauthenticated remote command-execution / auth-bypass mechanism
Gives a concrete immediate containment (restrict/deny management-interface access)
Recommends upgrading to a fixed BIG-IP version as the durable fix
Grounds urgency in KEV / active exploitation of a perimeter device
